skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Browser automation for AI agents via inference.sh. Navigate web pages, interact with elements using @e refs, take screenshots, record video. Capabilities: web scraping, form filling, clicking, typing, drag-drop, file upload, JavaScript execution. Use for: web automation, data extraction, testing, agent browsing, research. Triggers: browser, web automation, scrape, navigate, click, fill form, screenshot, browse web, playwright, headless browser, web agent, surf internet, record video
skill.summary: Headless browser automation for AI agents. Navigate pages, interact with elements, take screenshots, and record video sessions.
skill.author: inference-skills

security_audit.summary: This skill is a legitimate browser automation wrapper around Playwright via the inference.sh service. The static findings are primarily false positives from shell script patterns and legitimate browser automation features (cookie access, local storage). The skill requires the external `belt` CLI tool and makes network requests to inference.sh. No malicious intent detected - all code is documentation and shell script templates for web automation workflows.

medium_findings.title: Credential Access via Cookie Extraction
medium_findings.description: The skill documents accessing browser cookies via document.cookie JavaScript execution for authentication state extraction. While legitimate for browser automation, this pattern accesses sensitive credential data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Cookie access is a standard legitimate browser automation pattern for session reuse, not credential harvesting

medium_findings.title: Proxy Support with Authentication
medium_findings.description: The skill supports proxy servers with username/password authentication, which could be used to route traffic through potentially malicious proxies.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Proxy support is legitimate functionality but routing traffic through untrusted proxies poses security risks

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: Multiple hardcoded URLs in documentation examples (inference.sh, example.com, google.com). These are example URLs used in documentation, not actual network exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Hardcoded URLs in documentation examples are standard practice and not security concerns

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Sequence in Links
low_findings.description: The scanner detected '../' path sequences. These are standard markdown relative link syntax used in documentation files (e.g., [SKILL.md](../SKILL.md)), not actual path traversal vulnerabilities.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Markdown documentation uses '../' for relative links - this is documentation syntax, not a security vulnerability

low_findings.title: Video Recording May Capture Sensitive Data
low_findings.description: The video recording feature can capture sensitive information displayed during browser sessions. The skill documentation acknowledges this risk and recommends disabling recording in production.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Video capture is documented as a potential information disclosure risk with appropriate warnings

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Execution Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill executes shell commands via the 'belt' CLI tool. This is intentional design for browser automation, not arbitrary command injection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill wraps Playwright via the belt CLI - command execution is the intended mechanism, not a vulnerability

content.user_title: Automate browser tasks with AI agents
content.value_statement: Automate web interactions for AI agents. Navigate pages, fill forms, extract data, and record sessions without manual browsing. Eliminates repetitive web tasks and enables scalable browser automation.
actual_capabilities: Navigate to URLs and capture page screenshots
Interact with page elements using @e reference system (click, fill, drag, upload)
Execute JavaScript code on web pages
Record browser sessions as video for debugging and documentation
Handle authentication flows including OAuth, 2FA, and session reuse
Route traffic through proxy servers for geo-testing or network isolation
limitations: Requires inference.sh account and belt CLI installation
Cannot automate CAPTCHAs or advanced bot detection systems
Video recording may capture sensitive data if not properly managed
Session state does not persist across server restarts

use_cases.title: Automated web research and data extraction
use_cases.description: Use AI agents to browse multiple websites, extract product information, pricing data, or research content. AI agents can navigate complex sites, fill search forms, and collect structured data.
use_cases.target_user: Researchers, data analysts, and product teams gathering market intelligence

use_cases.title: End-to-end testing and screenshot documentation
use_cases.description: Create automated test scripts that verify web application functionality. Capture screenshots and video recordings of user flows for documentation or bug reporting.
use_cases.target_user: QA engineers and developers testing web applications

use_cases.title: Automated form submissions and workflow processing
use_cases.description: Automate repetitive form filling tasks like job applications, lead submissions, or data entry. Handle multi-step forms with validation and error recovery.
use_cases.target_user: Business analysts, recruiters, and operations teams processing online forms

prompt_templates.title: Basic page navigation and screenshot
prompt_templates.prompt: Open https://example.com and take a screenshot of the homepage showing all visible navigation elements.

prompt_templates.title: Form filling with submission
prompt_templates.prompt: Navigate to the contact form on https://example.com/contact. Fill in the name field with 'John Doe', email with 'john@example.com', and the message field with 'Hello, I need help with your product.' Then click the submit button and confirm the submission was successful.

prompt_templates.title: Authenticated data extraction workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Login to https://example.com with the credentials from environment variables (EMAIL and PASSWORD). After login, navigate to the dashboard, extract all visible project names and their status, take a full-page screenshot, then close the session.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced - authenticated automation with data extraction

prompt_templates.title: Multi-page research with recording
prompt_templates.prompt: Start a new browser session with video recording enabled. Search for 'best coding tools 2024' on Google, visit the top 3 results, extract key information from each page (headings, main features, pricing), and record the entire workflow for review.
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert - research workflow with video documentation

output_examples.input: Open https://example.com and get the page elements
Fill and submit the login form with user@example.com and password123
output_examples.output:
Action completed successfully.

- Filled input @e1 with 'user@example.com'
- Filled input @e2 with 'password123'
- Clicked button @e3 'Sign In'
- Waited 2000ms for redirect

Page now shows dashboard with welcome message. Session authenticated.

best_practices: Always use environment variables for credentials instead of hardcoding passwords in scripts
Re-snapshot page elements after any navigation or DOM changes to get fresh @e references
Enable video recording only during debugging; disable in production to avoid capturing sensitive data
anti_patterns: Do not store session IDs in version control or share them between unrelated workflows
Avoid automating CAPTCHA or anti-bot systems that may result in account bans
Do not leave sessions open indefinitely; always close them when work is complete to free server resources

faq.question: What is the difference between 'fill' and 'type' actions?
faq.answer: The 'fill' action clears the input field before typing, while 'type' appends text without clearing. Use 'fill' for form fields that may have pre-filled or default values.

faq.question: Why do element references (@e1, @e2) become invalid?
faq.answer: Element refs are tied to the page snapshot at a specific moment. After clicking links, submitting forms, or loading dynamic content, the DOM changes and refs become stale. Always call 'snapshot' to get fresh refs.

faq.question: How do I handle 2FA or multi-factor authentication?
faq.answer: For TOTP codes, you can use tools like oathtool to generate codes from secrets. For SMS or hardware tokens, enable video recording so users can see the prompt and manually enter codes.

faq.question: Can I run multiple browser sessions at once?
faq.answer: Yes, you can run multiple independent sessions simultaneously. Each session maintains its own cookies, storage, and browser state. Use unique session IDs to manage them separately.

faq.question: What happens if I do not close a session?
faq.answer: Sessions consume server resources on inference.sh. Always close sessions when done using the 'close' function. Use trap handlers in bash scripts to ensure cleanup even if errors occur.

faq.question: How do I extract data from a page after interacting with it?
faq.answer: Use the 'execute' function to run JavaScript that extracts data, or use 'snapshot' to get element text. You can also use 'screenshot' with full_page option to capture entire pages for analysis.
